data.title: AI to Drive Surge in Memory Prices Through 2026
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The expansion of AI will push memory prices up by as much as 20% next year, even after soaring in 2025, according to market research firm Counterpoint.

Memory prices are likely to rise 30% in Q4 2025 and possibly another 20% early next year, following 50% price increase this year, according to the company.

One of the key issues is a dearth of legacy LPDDR4 memory after suppliers like SK Hynix and Micron shifted output to more advanced high-bandwidth memory (HBM) to meet AI demand, Counterpoint said in a research report.

Spot-price imbalances are occurring, with DDR5 for servers and PCs trading at around $1.50 per gigabit, while older DDR4 used in consumer electronics fetches $2.10—higher than that of even advanced HBM3e, which is hovering around $1.70, the report said. 

Counterpoint, which forecasts DRAM production increases exceeding 20% in 2026, says suppliers are racing to meet demand.

“Samsung may reallocate its expanding 1C process, SK Hynix is pushing production and raising sales targets, CXMT may surprise on the upside and Micron, typically disciplined on return on investment, is unlikely to hold back,” Counterpoint Research Director MS Hwang said in the report. “The bigger risk on the horizon is with advanced memory as Nvidia’s recent pivot to LPDDR means it is a customer on the scale of a major smartphone maker—a seismic shift for the supply chain which can’t easily absorb this scale of demand.”

Record performance

SK Hynix, the world’s largest HBM supplier by sales, reported its highest quarterly performance in its most recent earnings results in October, driven by soaring prices of DRAM and NAND, as well as increasing shipments of HBM products for AI servers.

SK Hynix briefly eclipsed Samsung this year as the world’s largest memory maker on the strength of SK Hynix’s HBM sales. During the second half of 2025, Samsung regained its title as the leader in overall sales revenue.

“As the AI market rapidly shifts toward inference-driven workloads, there is growing interest in distributing computational loads of AI servers across broader infrastructures such as general servers,” SK Hynix said in its October earnings statement. “This trend is expected to further expand demand across the entire memory portfolio, including high-performance DDR5 and eSSD.”

The company plans to speed migration to its most advanced 1c DRAM process, a 10-nm node, that is in production. The shift toward the finer geometry will allow the company to establish a “full” DRAM lineup across server, mobile and graphics applications, according to SK Hynix.

The company’s leading HBM4 line will start shipping in the fourth quarter this year.

Amid surging demand for AI memory, SK Hynix said it has “secured full customer demand” for its DRAM and NAND production next year.

Spreading pain

The spike in memory prices is initially impacting smartphone makers, but others in the electronics ecosystem are at risk, according to Counterpoint.

“The pain could spread across the broader smartphone and consumer electronics ecosystem,” said Counterpoint Senior Analyst Ivan Lam in the report. “We are talking here about big increases to smartphone BoMs—upwards of 25% in the case of some models—across the key mid-to-high-end segments, eating into margins or affecting growth. It will probably be both.”

The industry faces constrained capacity and surging prices, which will force “painful” trade-offs for suppliers and manufacturers alike, the report said.

data.title: Ainekko Buys Esperanto Hardware IP, Open-Sources It
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Open-source AI hardware and software startup Ainekko has acquired the IP and selected assets of AI chip startup Esperanto Technologies, including its chip designs, software tooling, and development framework. Ainekko plans to open-source Esperanto’s production-grade many-core RISC-V architecture, including RTL, reference designs and development tools.

Ainekko started as a software layer for inference and distributed inference, but the founders quickly realized that without a co-optimized hardware backend, improvements to software were limited, Ainekko co-founder Tanya Dadasheva told EE Times.

“We always wanted flexibility,” Dadasheva said. “We believe [hardware] should be understandable by people in the open-source [software] ecosystem, they should be able to reason about it, and it should be flexible enough for people to do stuff with it.”

Esperanto, founded in 2014, had released a 1088-core RISC-V chip for hyperscale data centers’ recommendation workloads in 2021. The company pivoted toward HPC and generative AI in 2023, but ultimately found the hyperscale market difficult to break into and suffered from the loss of its European design team.

Dadasheva had worked with Esperanto in her previous role at Almaz Capital and had initially intended for Ainekko to partner with Esperanto, running Ainekko’s software stack on Esperanto hardware. When it became clear Esperanto wasn’t sustainable as a long-term partner, Ainekko decided acquiring the IP would be the best course of action.

Ainekko’s team has been working with Esperanto’s chips for about six months, Dadasheva said, and they have llama.cpp up and running, as well as the tinygrad framework. Some of Esperanto’s current-generation many-core chips will be used to bootstrap community initiatives, including FFmpeg work.

“Some people might come up with ideas of how to use it, beyond what we do as a business,” Dadasheva said. “Some people want to run shaders on it and try to do an open GPU type of solution, [for example], but one of the most interesting use cases for this is to use it as a full system-on-chip, without a host, in edge use cases.”

Esperanto had focused on hyperscale data centers, later pivoting slightly toward HPC with its 1000-core design. Ainekko expects new use cases for the technology to be focused on the edge, where power efficiency is critical. The architecture suits most edge AI applications, including industrial, robotics and drones, security systems, and embedded devices of all kinds.

“We are not trying to go for HPC and training, that is out of the question, but even in the inference space we don’t want to go for the data center, at least not for the next few years,” Dadasheva said.

Esperanto’s many-core RISC-V design made a good AI chip, but it will make an excellent general-purpose compute platform, Ainekko cofounder Roman Shaposhnik told EE Times.

“That’s exactly the match that we have with open-source, because open-source always goes for generality, even at the risk of losing some performance numbers, generality always wins,” he said.

Industrial PCs could be an interesting use case for Ainekko hardware and software, according to Shaposhnik, especially given the sizable total addressable market (TAM). Another area of interest is smart interposers for SSD interfaces which use AI to look for abnormal reads and writes, killing the system if it detects a security breach. No SoCs exist for this use case today, Shaposhnik said.

Silicon plans

Ainekko is making plans for its own first silicon. The company is planning a tapeout with just eight Esperanto cores (a single Esperanto “neighborhood” – Esperanto’s own design was 1088 cores), which it plans to attach to 16 MB of MRAM from startup Veevx (Veevx cofounder and CEO, Broadcom veteran Doug Smith, is also Ainekko’s third cofounder). This tapeout will go onto an imminent TSMC shuttle wafer.

Next on the roadmap will be an intermediate-size design in the region of 256 cores, Dadasheva said, coming closer in performance to the quad-Arm-Cortex-A76 processor on the Raspberry Pi 5, but more optimized for AI inference.

“We are not afraid to open [the hardware IP] to the community, but as a business, we want to offer something like a Raspberry Pi 5 that could be used for prototyping,” Dadasheva said.

Cloud access to the company’s existing Esperanto cards is already available in order to help potential users evaluate how many cores they need, amongst other design parameters. Esperanto’s emulator and software stack has already been open-sourced, and the community is already getting comfortable working with these technologies, Dadasheva said.

“We have seen a lot of interest from academia to take Esperanto cores and start experimenting with them, not necessarily taping them out, but putting them on an FPGA and reasoning about them,” Dadasheva said. “If it suddenly takes off and people tape it out, that’s a really good thing because it will increase our software footprint.”

Anyone taping out Esperanto-based hardware would not necessarily need to use Ainekko software, but the company’s stack will be optimized directly for the hardware IP and the two will evolve together, Dadasheva said. Concerns about working with startup IP are mitigated by showing Esperanto’s existing silicon and allowing complete access to inspect and download the code, she added. There are also plans afoot to make the hardware IP available via embedded chiplet platform ZeroASIC, she said.

Ainekko’s hardware IP and software has been open-sourced under the AI Foundry brand, which is not currently a foundation, but it could be, Dadasheva said. Discussions about a potential foundation will continue into next year.

Ainekko plans to eventually provide ASIC design services to potential customers. There are also possibilities for hybrid relationships with companies that are taping out their own chips, including sales partnerships, Shaposhnik said.

“Nobody has really tried to run an open-source RTL design fully in the open,” Shaposhnik said. “But we’re at the point where it will be possible to collaborate on smaller designs on higher-level nodes. It’s a big question mark; prerequisites to that obviously include our actual RTL being available in the open, but also tools, at least on the front end of the process for design and emulation.”

data.title: D-Wave Among Few Ramping Quantum Computer Sales
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

D-Wave chief development officer Trevor Lanting told EE Times in an exclusive interview that the company sees sales of quantum computing systems increasing. D-Wave and IBM are the only quantum companies in that category so far.

D-Wave stole a lead on rivals by being the first to introduce its quantum-annealing tech, with a shorter path to commercialization. Now D-Wave is doing the hard part: the gate-model devices that it and competitors may need about seven years to bring to production.

In the meantime, D-Wave is adding sales of hardware to its bread-and-butter quantum-as-a-service business.

“Over the last year, there’s been a growing interest in on-premises quantum infrastructure and quantum-computing systems,” Lanting said. “At the end of 2024, we closed a sale of our Advantage system to the Jülich Supercomputing Center, and we deployed that system earlier in 2025.”

The aim is to connect the quantum system to Europe’s Jupiter exascale computer, enabling breakthroughs in AI and quantum optimization. The project would be the world’s first coupling of an annealing quantum computer with an exascale supercomputer. D-Wave has also installed systems at the University of Southern California’s Information Sciences Institute; in Huntsville, Alabama, with Davidson Technologies; and in Italy for the Q-Alliance, according to Murray Thom, D-Wave’s VP of quantum technology evangelism.

“In Davidson’s case, they want to work on a set of important defense applications, and you need a specific IT context to do that,” Thom said. “They want to set that up to enable the defense industry to be able to take advantage of the technology. In the case of the Q-Alliance, the Italian government wants to take an advanced position in the knowledge economy, and that is about workforce upskilling, advanced research and enabling companies to get the productivity, efficiency and agility that quantum-computing technology is providing today.”

D-Wave’s annealing quantum computing tech is designed specifically to solve optimization problems by using quantum mechanics to find the lowest energy state of a system. The quantum computer starts in a superposition of all possible solutions and evolves toward a ground state, which represents the optimal solution.

In March, D-Wave claimed to be first in quantum supremacy when it published a peer-reviewed article in Science demonstrating how its annealing quantum computer performed in minutes magnetic materials simulations that would have taken nearly one million years and more than the world’s annual electricity supply to solve using the Frontier supercomputer at the Department of Energy’s Oak Ridge National Laboratory.

Gate-model tech

Earlier this year, D-Wave said it would pivot to development of the more challenging gate-model technology that IBM, Google, Rigetti Computing and Quantinuum have been working on.

D-Wave saw no commercially viable path toward a gate-model tech 15-plus years ago, but two critical innovations in the intervening years changed the company’s thinking. The first is error-correction methodology, finding a way to do encoding and error correction with gate-model architectures. The second change is fundamental innovation in qubit design.

“We see a path toward a large-scale gate-model technology,” Lanting noted. “Based on superconducting approaches, our gate-model qubits are our so-called fluxonium qubits. They’re very similar to the qubits that are in our annealing processor. Parametrically a little bit different, but we can adapt the electronics, the shielding, the cryogenic control and the fabrication that we’ve built up for the annealing technology. We see a lot of fundamental synergy between the two technology development efforts.”

All the quantum industry’s gate-model efforts will probably take somewhere from seven to 15 years of sophisticated R&D to reach a scale where the work yields commercial applications, Thom said.

“Our customers want us to help make sense for them on that spectrum of applications between what’s immediately available, what’s transformative and revolutionary now and in the future, what’s next,” Thom noted. “With gate model, you’re not targeting the same applications. It’s not going to be efficient for optimization, but if you want to do molecular-simulation calculations, that’s the only way to do it.”

During 2025, D-Wave had more than 100 revenue-generating customers, including about 20 in the Forbes Global 2,000. The company sells quantum services via its Leap Cloud Platform to companies like MasterCard, Ford and Volkswagen.

SkyWater partnership

D-Wave plans to continue its engagement with SkyWater as its chip foundry for both annealing and gate-based quantum processors, Lanting said.

“The qubits are manufactured and embedded in this multi-layer fabrication stack,” Lanting noted. “Skywater makes the chip that’s at the core of the system, a large superconducting integrated circuit. A key to our success in getting to the 5000-qubit scale is that we have on-chip digital analog converters. We can control a very large number of these DACs with a very small number of lines because we have on-chip demultiplexing. A good chunk of the complexity is involved with the digital control, but all the key bits are also manufactured and embedded at the same time within the same integrated stack.”

A critical part of D-Wave’s gate-model development is the scalable control circuitry, Lanting added.

“That’s definitely something we will be leveraging Skywater to produce,” he noted. “Skywater remains critical to that development program.”

data.title: Crypto Mining ASIC Goes Deep Sub-Threshold On 3 nm
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Japanese crypto-mining ASIC company Triple-1 is about to tape out its third-generation Bitcoin-mining chip, Kamikaze III, built on TSMC 3-nm process technology. Kamikaze III will offer power efficiency of 10.45 J/TH, thanks to proprietary techniques that enable sub-threshold operation, and the company plans to use the same technology in a forthcoming AI accelerator chip, Triple-1 president and CEO Takuya Yamaguchi told EE Times in an exclusive interview.

Yamaguchi founded Triple-1 in 2016 and the company launched its first generation Kamikaze crypto-mining chip in 2018 on TSMC 7 nm. A second-generation crypto-mining chip was launched in 2020 alongside a test chip for an in-house AI accelerator, both on TSMC 5 nm.

The first generation of the Kamikaze chip was launched in 2018. Crypto-mining chips became a difficult market, Yamaguchi said, because of the downturn in cryptocurrencies’ value. Triple-1 started working with the leading Japanese utility Tepco (Tokyo Electric Power Company) in 2021.

Following the Fukushima nuclear disaster, Japanese utilities invested heavily in renewable energy generation—primarily solar—but renewables can be unpredictable as they are often weather-dependent. Excess energy supply can either be stored or wasted, and in 2018, it was generally wasted as battery costs were high, Yamaguchi said.

Triple-1’s collaboration with Tepco led the company down two paths: the company teamed up with Tepco subsidiary Agile Energy to deploy crypto-mining servers at the edge, close to where the energy is generated but used only when there is an energy surplus, and simultaneously developed a solid-state battery product.

“We do not only focus on semiconductor design,” Yamaguchi said. “As we look at the market, everything is strongly connected: power generation, infrastructure and transmission, energy storage and supply—they are all linked to the data center.”

Energy storage

“Fluctuating energy supply is a huge problem for all electricity companies in the world,” Yamaguchi added. “High-performance batteries which are safe and cheap will be critical in the future for a safer, more stable electricity market.”

The company has a separate in-house team working on solid-state battery development. Triple-1’s solid state battery achieves energy density above 280 Wh/kg.

“We have a deep discussion with not just Tokyo Electric, but also outside of Japanese electricity companies and governments to utilize this battery,” Yamaguchi said.

Japan’s electricity grid, unlike Western “mesh” grids, has a “fishbone” structure, with side lines coming out of a main trunk. Fishbone grids in particular suffer from congestion, but both grid topologies have thinner electricity cables in suburban areas, which are not suitable for demand on the grid and transmission from suburban solar installations.

“Investment into this cable is quite expensive for electricity companies,” he said. “To store energy in the local area, or to run the data center [on surplus energy], can add value .… because of that, there is a good fit to collaborate with these players.”

Triple-1 wants to bring its battery and its edge crypto-mining servers to governments and utilities in Asia and around the world.

Power efficiency

Crypto-mining server deployment at the edge relies on power efficiency. Triple-1 achieves this by working on the most advanced process nodes available, as well as employing proprietary sub-threshold design techniques.

Kamikaze III will be built on TSMC 3 nm. Being Japanese gives Triple-1 an advantage as the company is freely able to access these advanced process nodes (a majority of the crypto ASIC market share is held by Bitmain, a Chinese company, and its affiliate Sophgo, who have been denied access to TSMC since Sophgo chips were found in Huawei equipment).  

“Triple-1 is the only player outside of China who has succeeded in developing this kind of chip,” Yamaguchi said. “Even the big U.S. players couldn’t make it happen. So, we have a certain uniqueness in the market.”

Crypto-mining chips are a popular choice for foundries as a “pipe-cleaner,” the first products to be produced using a new process node. This is partly because crypto-mining chips do not have any high-speed interface IP, codec IP or SRAM, which need to be verified and tested separately. This type of chip has a relatively simple architecture with a single clock. They are also relatively small, high-yield, high-volume parts. This helps companies like Triple-1 get early access to new process nodes.

Triple-1 plans to collaborate with fledgeling Japanese foundry Rapidus on its advanced process nodes in the next couple of years, Yamaguchi said, as a way to offer a Japan-only supply chain to customers as an option.

Sub-threshold techniques

Triple-1 has used sub-threshold operation in multiple generations of its crypto-mining chips. For Kamikaze III, Triple-1’s target operating point is 0.26 V.

Sub-threshold design is beyond the limits of automated EDA tools today. Design is therefore enabled by Triple-1’s additional characterization of TSMC’s PDK, custom cell design and manual routing, which together are a significant part of the company’s IP.

“The strength of our technology lies in its ability to minimize power consumption and cost while controlling risk,” Yamaguchi said. “After ensuring chip functionality, our highest priority is low power consumption, followed by cost, and finally, temperature. We will select air cooling for the final system if the thermal requirements are not extremely strict. If the thermal requirements are exceptionally demanding, we will opt for liquid cooling to ensure the chip always operates correctly.”

Other temperature mitigation techniques include low-power modes like sleep and deep sleep modes, which can expand the chip’s environmental temperature range. Specific operating modes can be introduced during the design phase if the likely environmental temperature profile is known. Advanced techniques, like software-cooperative design where temperature is sensed and fed back to enable dynamic switching of operating modes, are also possible.

Sub-threshold has become a viable technique as crypto-mining chips have reduced in size (since larger chips restrict clock speed at sub-threshold voltages), Yamaguchi said, noting that multiple small chips are used on each board.

Next gen AI

Could Triple-1’s sub-threshold techniques be applied to future AI chips? And could AI chips be deployed at the edge, in or near solar farms, to generate LLM tokens with surplus energy?

In fact, many crypto-mining companies are already changing their focus to AI, Yamaguchi said. (For example, cloud company IREN is transitioning from a crypto-mining company to an AI cloud company, and recently signed a $9.7 billion deal with Microsoft.)

“[Crypto-mining] players are working to start collaborating with U.S. hyperscalers,” Yamaguchi said. “There’s one critical reason: the electricity contract [crypto-mining companies] have with local electricity companies.”

Today, 10-12 GW of capacity, or about 15 % of the entire data center industry’s power footprint, is used to power crypto mining. These companies have already negotiated favorable prices from their electricity suppliers, which look very attractive to AI companies, Yamaguchi said.

Triple-1’s existing relationships with crypto mining companies, combined with its distributed computing approach and access to an otherwise overlooked source of power, could be a competitive advantage for future engagements with AI companies, he added.

“We are focusing at this moment on the mining side, not AI, but once the market requires it, we can easily shift to AI,” Yamaguchi said. “Suburban data centers are in the near future for AI, not a centralized GPU-based hyperscale data center. To make AI services available to the entire country, we need to deploy locally.”

Triple-1 built a 5-nm AI accelerator test chip, Goku, in 2020. This chip used an in-house designed training NPU, achieving 10 TFLOPS/W at FP16, but did not use the company’s sub-threshold design expertise. However, these technologies could be combined for a future AI chip, especially if the apparent synergy between sub-threshold’s reliability and AI inference’s lower precision requirements could be exploited.

For future applications like AI chips, sub-threshold operation could be applied only to certain blocks on the chip to mitigate issues with clock speed, Yamaguchi said.

Another issue for sub-threshold is SRAM, which is notoriously unstable at low voltage. Kamikaze crypto-mining chips do not use SRAM, in part because use of very advanced process nodes meant SRAM had not been fully characterized for the target process node. In any case, this problem has been greatly reduced for modern finFETs, Yamaguchi said, noting that the company does not expect this to be an issue for future sub-threshold designs, including SRAM.

Challenges for a future Triple-1 AI chip include interconnect. Like AI chips, crypto-mining chips are many-core, though the cores work independently to perform the same hash calculation. This is the opposite of an AI accelerator where cores need to work together on a single, large problem. Thus, while core-to-core and chip-to-chip interconnect is not crucial for crypto-mining, it would be for an AI accelerator.

Triple-1 has commercial edge AI inference chips on its roadmap, Yamaguchi said, but whether AI token generation makes sense for distributed computing is still under discussion.

In the mean time, Triple-1 will seek to commercialize its distributed computing infrastructure concept with governments and utilities in Asia and further afield, either by selling chips or by working with partners to provide servers or higher-level solutions, Yamaguchi said.

data.title: GF Targets $1 Billion Silicon Photonics Revenue with AMF Acquisition
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

GlobalFoundries (GF) today announced the acquisition of Advanced Micro Foundry (AMF), a silicon photonics foundry based in Singapore, claiming this makes GF the world’s largest pure-play silicon photonics foundry. GF said AMF will add over $75 million to its revenue in 2026, and the company expects its annual silicon photonics revenue to be in excess of $1 billion by the end of the decade.

The value of the deal was not disclosed, but GF expects AMF’s manufacturing assets, extensive IP and skilled talent to significantly expand GF’s silicon photonics technology and market. In the official announcement, GF said it is leveraging over 15 years of AMF’s manufacturing expertise to address demands in long-haul optical communications, computing, LiDAR and sensing on AMF’s 200-mm platform in Singapore. The company also has plans to scale to 300 mm as market needs grow, “ensuring reliable global supply for AI datacenters, communications and next-generation applications,” according to GF.

GF said that as it continues to expand its U.S. manufacturing capacity for silicon photonics in New York, this acquisition of AMF would accelerate the ramp of its Singapore operation. GF also plans to establish a silicon photonics research and development center of excellence in Singapore, partnering with the Agency for Science, Technology and Research (A*STAR), Singapore’s lead public sector R&D agency, to advance GF’s innovation roadmap by focusing on next-generation materials for ultra-fast data transfer at 400Gbps speed.

AMF is a specialty silicon photonics foundry offering a full spectrum of manufacturing, prototyping and testing services, supported by proprietary technology platforms. It uses CMOS-compatible processes and a proven 200-mm production line, which the company says delivers high-yield, cost-efficient photonic integrated circuits.

For its prototyping services, AMF states it offers prototyping services and multi-wafer project shuttles on multiple platforms—silicon-on-insulator (SOI) as well silicon nitride on SOI—AMF also offers wafer level packaging solutions.

GF CEO Tim Breen said in a LinkedIn post today, “This move fits perfectly with our decade-long strategy in silicon photonics—a technology that’s now critical for powering the data-driven world, from cloud data centers to the physical AI systems at the edge.”

In an official announcement Breen said, “Acquiring AMF enables GF to deliver an expanded, and differentiated, decade-long roadmap for pluggable transceivers and co-packaged optics, while accelerating growth of photonics into adjacent markets such as automotive and quantum computing.” 

Silicon photonics is a must have

Silicon photonics is now widely expected to address some of the limitations in AI data center infrastructure. As traditional copper connections reach their physical limits, silicon photonics has been emerging as a potential breakthrough technology by using light to transfer data between and within data centers, delivering ultra-fast, energy-efficient performance.

“Silicon photonics technology is essential for AI infrastructure,” Breen said. “As data moves faster and workloads grow more complex, the ability to move information with greater speed, precision and power efficiency is now fundamental to AI datacenters and advanced telecom networks.”

The opportunity for optical networking in data center infrastructure. (Source: GlobalFoundries)

The silicon photonics opportunity. (Source: GlobalFoundries)

According to a report released in May 2025 by analyst firm LightCounting, silicon photonics is now a “must have technology.” The report notes that despite many advantages of silicon photonics technology, it took almost a decade for it to make an impact on the optical transceiver market.

“Decisions made by several large companies, including Cisco, Huawei and Intel, helped to accelerate adoption of silicon photonics,” the report said. “We expect that use of linear drive pluggables (LPO) and co-packaged optics (CPO) will double the market share of this technology from 30% in 2025 to 60% in 2030, as illustrated in the figure below. Once again, several large companies, including Broadcom and Nvidia, will be enabling this transition.”

Silicon photonics transceivers market share. (Source: LightCounting).

LightCounting said the transition from pluggable to CPO is an exciting one for the industry, but it is important to set realistic expectations for the adoption of these solutions. Apart from numerous manufacturing challenges and meeting the targets set for lower power consumption, end users must accept CPO as a viable approach for continuing cost reduction.

Its report estimates the market value of optical chips used in transceivers, active optical cables, LPO and CPO shipped last year was a modest $1.7 billion, and silicon photonics chips account for about one third of that. The market is projected to triple in size, exceeding $5 billion in 2030 and the market share of silicon photonics is projected to double.

 “This is a 6× growth and the forecasted market value is a bit more exciting, but hardly enough for companies like TSMC. It must be the long-term strategy that attracts CMOS foundries to silicon photonics,” the report said. “Something along the lines of Intel’s vision for the last two decades: where the optical interconnects, such as CPO, become an essential technology for any complex ASIC to function. This vision may be only a decade away now and this is the right time to focus on silicon photonics. Intel might have been 20 years early, but no large CMOS foundry or ASIC vendor can afford to miss it now.”

data.title: Dutch Clampdown on Nexperia Puts Europe’s Chip Sovereignty Strategy to the Test
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

When the Dutch government invoked an emergency law in late September 2025 to take control of semiconductor maker Nexperia, it signaled a turning point in how Europe intends to defend its technological sovereignty. The action was justified as a matter of national security, but it is reverberating through the semiconductor supply chain—and the automotive industry, in particular, is feeling the tremors.

A rare move under the Goods Availability Act

Nexperia, headquartered in Nijmegen, Netherlands, is one of Europe’s largest producers of legacy chips—the diodes, MOSFETs and logic components that quietly power cars, industrial systems, and consumer electronics. The company, a former NXP Semiconductors unit, has been owned by China’s Wingtech Technology since 2019.

Citing “acute governance shortcomings” and “risks to the continuity and safeguarding of crucial technological capabilities,” the Dutch Ministry of Economic Affairs temporarily seized control of the firm under the Goods Availability Act, a Cold War–era law seldom used since the 1950s. The move suspended CEO Zhang Xuezheng (aka Wingtech Zhang) and granted the government a deciding vote over management decisions.

Beijing responded by curbing exports of components and tooling from Wingtech-controlled facilities in China, raising fears of shortages in Europe’s already-tight automotive supply chain.

The irony is that Nexperia does not make the advanced AI processors that dominate geopolitical headlines. It produces mature, high-volume chips on 8-inch wafers—devices often considered commodities. Yet those parts are the backbone of Europe’s car manufacturing sector, where hundreds of small, validated components come together under strict qualification and safety rules. That is what makes the disruption so serious, said Jo Van Biesebroeck, professor of economics at KU Leuven.

“Changing a supplier in the automotive industry takes time,” he told EE Times in an interview. “Vehicle components are interdependent, so any change has ripple effects. Coordinating sourcing adjustments can take more than six months.”

Even seemingly simple substitutions can set off a cascade of redesigns, retesting and re-certifications. For Tier 1 suppliers and car OEMs already stretched by electrification and software-defined-vehicle projects, the timing could hardly be worse.

According to Van Biesebroeck, automakers usually hedge risk at the corporate level but not at the product level. “OEMs dual-source in order not to be overly reliant on one supplier,” he said. “But at the model level, they often single-source. Changes in sourcing strategy therefore affect the production of individual models greatly.”

The Nexperia case underscores the growing tension between industrial policy and manufacturing reality. Europe wants tighter control of semiconductor production on its soil, but its automotive sector depends on long-established supply relationships—many involving global firms and Chinese partners.

Policy meets production reality

According to Benedetta Girardi, strategic analyst at the Hague Centre for Strategic Studies, the national-security dimension goes beyond supply continuity. “Governance failures were the formal explanation,” she told EE Times in an interview. “But another pressing issue was the proposal to shift essential Dutch operations and decision-making authority to China. The security implications extended beyond foreign ownership to the possible dilution of operational control.

“The Netherlands is a classically liberal market economy,” she added. “So, this was really an experiment—a way to see how far a middle power like the Netherlands can push with a great power like China. I don’t think there’s a desire to escalate; both sides have strong incentives to de-escalate once they’ve tested the limits.”

On the European level, the Commission’s 2023 Economic Security Strategy explicitly called for “protective instruments” in sensitive technologies. Over the past two years, several EU member states have tightened foreign investment screening, mirroring the logic of the U.S. Committee on Foreign Investment. Germany blocked a Chinese investor from acquiring Elmos Semiconductor’s fab in Dortmund; France scrutinized Atos’s disposal of its HPC arm; and Italy has expanded its “golden power” regime for chip assets.

The Dutch intervention fits this broader European pattern. But their move goes further than screening; it amounts to direct operational control. Girardi said the combination of the 1952 Goods Availability Act and the Vifo Act of 2023 creates a new hybrid toolkit.

“Emergency powers definitely had a big role to play,” she said. “The Vifo Act handles the screening side, but the Goods Availability Act gives the state operational control in crisis. The precedent is that Europe can now move from ex ante investment screening to ex post governance intervention, but we shouldn’t be alarmist. This will stay limited to truly critical technologies.”

Girardi noted that Beijing’s reaction so far has been measured: “China has bigger fish to fry than the Netherlands and Nexperia. It’s likely to delay rather than disrupt. Beijing knows that escalation would hurt its own supply chain.”

According to Van Biesebroeck, firms increasingly perceive these flareups as part of a larger pattern. “Actions like this are part of the ongoing reorganization of supply chains,” he said. “Firms now experience the environment as pushing them toward reshoring, shortening supply chains and ensuring supply locally.”

For Europe’s automotive sector, the episode serves as a reminder that “legacy” does not mean “low risk.” Van Biesebroeck said he believes governments and manufacturers alike are only starting to grasp the strategic importance of mature-node chips.

“Many products could be considered strategic, especially if country partnerships become less reliable,” he said. “It would be useful for governments to map which materials and technologies belong on that list. My guess is that legacy chips would be on it.”

Girardi expects the short-term impact to show up mainly in delays. “We’ll probably see spikes in lead times and some component shortages,” she said. “Modules like power management and sensors could be affected, but I don’t expect complete interruptions, just delays that cascade through the supply chain.”

The wider stakes for Europe’s Chips Act

The Dutch action comes just as the EU Chips Act moves from planning to implementation. Its €43 billion (approximately $49.8 billion) funding framework aims to double Europe’s global semiconductor market share by 2030, emphasizing both cutting-edge nodes and the mature technologies that underpin automotive and industrial systems.

Girardi situated the Nexperia move within the broader EU “de-risking” agenda. “Brussels backed Dutch policymakers but didn’t coordinate the decision,” she said. “The EU may push for minimum standards for emergency intervention or cross-border coordination, but it has no legal base for an EU-wide framework for operational control. Semiconductors are still a private industry.”

Negotiations between The Hague and Beijing are ongoing, with reports suggesting the Netherlands could relinquish control if Chinese export restrictions are lifted and new governance safeguards are agreed. For now, Nexperia’s operations continue, but the message to industry is clear: Europe is ready to act decisively when it feels its technological base is at risk.

“The gap between business and government is closing,” Girardi said. “Not because business will be managed by the state but because geopolitics has become part of corporate strategy. Companies need to plan governance that anticipates possible state intervention. Independent local boards are a good start.”

By “independent local boards,” Girardi means governance bodies based in the host country, such as the Netherlands for Nexperia, composed of directors not tied to the foreign parent company. Their role is to ensure transparency and reassure governments that management decisions and sensitive operations are conducted under proper local oversight, reducing the risk of state intervention.

Whether that stance strengthens Europe’s semiconductor ecosystem or chills investment will depend on how deftly policymakers balance sovereignty with openness. What is certain is that the semiconductor industry, long a quiet enabler of globalization, has become a frontline of geopolitics.

For automakers, chip suppliers and governments alike, the Nexperia case may mark the moment that Europe’s chip sovereignty ambitions became an industrial reality—with all the complications that entails.

See also:

Europe’s Nexperia Move: A Self-Inflicted Supply Shock

Europe’s Chips Act Stumbles Over Its Own Rigidity

EE Times Europe Magazine: The Geopolitics of Semiconductors

data.title: Zetwerk Pursues Component-Focused Manufacturing Strategy Amid India’s EMS Push
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Indian electronics manufacturing services company, Zetwerk Electronics, is strengthening its position in India by diversifying its global supply chain and preparing for backward integration and design-led capabilities along with tightening lifecycle discipline.

In an exclusive conversation with EE Times, CEO Josh Foulger said that these priorities are central to staying relevant as the industry faces rapid technological shifts and rebalanced global manufacturing.

Zetwerk now spans “multiple verticals, from consumer to strategic electronics,” with India being the primary base. The company recently inaugurated a new factory in Chennai, which has already delivered a million units and shipped around four million devices to customers last month.

Zetwerk Electronics’ Chennai manufacturing unit. (Source: Zetwerk)

“We are proud participants of the Electronic Component Manufacturing Scheme, and the government’s positive response has been encouraging. These schemes have simplified processes and strengthened the partnership between government and industry. Looking ahead to 2030 and beyond, India is definitely on the right track,” Foulger said.

Speaking at the same panel, Ministry of Electronics and IT (MeitY) Joint Secretary, Sushil Pal, widely credited for driving the Electronics Components Manufacturing Scheme, pushed for local value addition. “Indian companies are now showcasing their capabilities …. designed for the world,” he said, adding that MeitY’s role is to enable growth while ensuring regulations do not hinder operations. “We are now focusing on building printed circuit boards, power electronics, and components locally so that value addition increases within the country.”

Manufacturing strategy

Zetwerk’s five-year plan focuses on building competence in components and design. “Our next step is backward integration into components, which is critical for cost and quality control,” he shared at a recent India Mobile Congress panel on advancing India’s manufacturing ambitions.

The company is also simultaneously investing in developing design solutions. “These initiatives will take time,” Foulger shared, “but the right balance between manufacturing, component integration and design will help us remain competitive and relevant.”

He acknowledged the “positive movement” in semiconductors and progress in programmable components and connectors. “Recently, we announced antenna manufacturing, not only for domestic consumption but also for exports, where we have been able to localize up to 65 to 70 percent,” he shared. “Many of the cases, enclosures and related parts have already been localized.”

Supply chain re-alignment

Foulger also shared that Zetwerk is responding to the ongoing “China plus one” shift shaping global EMS strategy. “At Zetwerk, we already follow what we call a blended supply chain,” he told EE Times. “We have factories in the United States, Europe and India, with supply bases spread across regions.”

Speaking about the scaling challenges that could hinder growth, Foulger clarified that most execution bottlenecks arise long before mass production. “Managing the complete product lifecycle is essential,” he said. “If products don’t reach ramp-up speed quickly enough, it leads to challenges like dead stock, unsold inventory, and wasted resources. At Zetwerk, we have standardized processes, trained teams and aligned functions across all our factories to maintain efficiency.”

He added that India’s electronics ecosystem is expanding rapidly across states. “In the next 36 months, we will see manufacturing hubs expand across Chennai, Bengaluru, NCR, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh and Andhra Pradesh,” he said. “Indian states are the size of many ASEAN countries, so the capacity and opportunities are almost limitless.”

The company’s blended supply chain structure allows it to create “the right blend” for each requirement. Resilience, he added, must be built at several levels—factory floors, sourcing strategies and the global network—“to de-risk operations.”

Foulger also called for systematic gap analysis and capacity building in component manufacturing. “We need to assess what is available in India right now, do a gap analysis to identify what more is needed, and then look at where investments, upgrades, or government interventions may be required,” he said. “The objective is to prepare those components locally within India.”

See also:

Global Component Makers Deepen India Ties Under New Manufacturing Push 

Aimtron’s Design-Led Approach Secures Manufacturing Wins

data.title: Are Synopsys Layoffs a Harbinger of the AI-Assisted Design Era?
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

While Synopsys’ lay off of 10% of its workforce amid its merger with Ansys is making waves in trade media, it has also sparked a broad debate about whether design automation and AI are also factors behind job cuts of this scale. EDA heavyweights like Synopsys are investing heavily in AI-first workflows that autonomously explore chip architectures, optimize layouts and reduce power leakage without manual tuning.

But let us first take a closer look at Synopsys’ $35 billion cash-and-stock acquisition of engineering design firm Ansys, and how this integration’s aim to create a leaner operating model is leading to nearly 2,000 well-paid job cuts.

The big cut

The merger between chip design toolmaker Synopsys and Ansys, specializing in multi-physics simulation software for aerospace, automotive and semiconductors, was aimed to create a chip-to-system design powerhouse.

However, the $35 billion takeover of Ansys comes with unintended consequences that management will face as it streamlines operations and eliminates functional overlaps.

In a way, the layoffs of this scale were inevitable when a large EDA firm was acquired by an even larger EDA outfit. In other words, the merger of two complex software portfolios and their corresponding organizational structures necessitated a major overhaul.

Factors beyond Ansys acquisition

The official version is that these layoffs are a necessary step to drive business efficiencies and redirect investment toward higher-growth areas. However, it is important to note that this workforce reduction has been on the cards since Synopsys’ earnings call in September 2025.

So, while the integration with Ansys is clearly a major catalyst, are there other factors at play in Synopsys’ largest workforce reduction in two decades? Industry watchers frequently point to Synopsys’ missing revenue estimates in the third quarter of 2025.

The Sunnyvale, California-based EDA house attributed the shortfall to a slowdown in China and a major foundry client setback. While the U.S. government lifted export restrictions on EDA tools in July 2025 after imposing them in May 2025, the six-week sanction had a severe impact on Synopsys’ balance sheet.

The sanction disrupted new chip design starts and made IC designers in China more cautious about their long-term commitments. China, Synopsys’ second-largest market after the U.S., still represented 14.2% of its sales in this quarter.

Besides China woes, Synopsys noted a slowdown in its design IP segment and difficulties faced by a major foundry client. According to a Reuters story, Synopsys invested significant resources to develop specialized IP for a foundry and was expecting returns in the second half of 2025.

However, this foundry client withdrew for several reasons, resulting in an 8% decline in IP business revenue at Synopsys. Though Synopsys has not named the foundry client, industry sources believe it is Intel, which has indicated plans to use the 18A process node primarily for internal purposes.

Are AI-driven EDA tools a factor?

Large acquisitions inevitably lead to job cuts, but with a workforce reduction of this scale, industry observers have started to contemplate whether the AI-based EDA platforms are also a factor. Especially when Synopsys posted an increase in design automation revenue of 23% in the third quarter, in which it missed revenue forecasts.

EDA toolmakers like Synopsys are shifting their focus toward greater automation, requiring fewer human engineers. These AI-driven EDA platforms redefine design efficiency and productivity by leveraging reinforcement learning and genAI capabilities to automate tasks like RTL generation and testbench creation.

Case in point: Synopsys.ai Copilot employs genAI capabilities in areas like formal assertion generation and RTL code generation to accelerate design and verification cycle times from days to hours. Moreover, design engineers can perform documentation searches and script generation in minutes instead of hours.

So, Synopsys, while boasting AI-driven productivity, could become an early manifestation of the much-talked-about premise that AI tools will replace human engineers. At the same time, however, it is not a full-fledged “AI takes human jobs” doomsday scenario. Apparently, design engineers are increasingly collaborating with AI platforms; they set the high-level design intent while AI handles the repetitive grunt work.

The dawn of a hybrid design era

EDA, once a near-monopoly with sticky revenues, is in the midst of a disruption driven by AI-assisted design toolsets. The mass layoffs at Synopsys could be a harbinger of a new era of semiconductor design driven by automation and AI-assisted toolsets.

That calls for design engineers to seek reinvention—a.k.a. skills reboot—at a time when traditional hierarchies of semiconductor design and verification are steadily disrupted. They must prepare for a hybrid setup in which they work in an AI-assisted environment to co-develop semiconductor products more efficiently and quickly.

More importantly, once the trickle-down effect of these AI-driven EDA toolsets reaches the doorstep of chip vendors, there could be a disruption on a much larger scale. So, design engineers must acquaint themselves with this new era of AI-driven semiconductor design sooner rather than later.

See also:

U.S. Restricts EDA Software Sales to China

And Finally: Synopsys Closes $35bn Purchase of Ansys

Synopsys: Autonomous AI Agents to Tame Chip Design Complexity

data.title: AI Chip Startup Tsavorite Emerges With $100 Million In Pre-Orders
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

AI chip startup Tsavorite Scalable Intelligence has emerged from stealth mode with $100 million in pre-orders for its chiplet-based AI inference accelerators.

Tsavorite has an FPGA prototype of its chiplets up and running today. Customers that have already signed up include Fortune Global 500 companies, sovereign cloud providers, as well as systems integrators in the U.S., Asia and Europe. In fact, the company has eight design-ins to date, worth a total of $350 million when the orders are placed, Shalesh Thusoo, founder and CEO of Tsavorite, told EE Times.

“Everyone wants more tokens per second for the new applications coming in, but power is limited,” Thusoo said. “The other thing is, AI has a lot of scalability requirements, depending on the application and the use cases, and a lot of solutions today are either scalable or efficient. Our architecture gets you the scalability without losing the efficiency.”

Founded in 2023, the company has developed two chiplets, which together make up a number of chip products suitable for applications between robotics and data centers.  

“Because of our chiplet modularity, we can serve different markets as things evolve, where someone wants more of one versus the other as far as memory bandwidth and scale,” Thusoo said.

MultiPlexus fabric

Tsavorite’s omni processing unit (OPU) chiplets have CPUs, AI accelerators, memory and scale up/out connected via its MultiPlexus fabric, with the bulk of the company’s secret sauce in the fabric design, Thusoo said. This fabric allows large scale-out designs up to 8,000 OPUs with unified memory, while maintaining low latency and high-memory bandwidth. MultiPlexus is coherent and scalable with distributed caches and security built into the fabric; additional network switches and NICs are not required.

“[Our fabric] saves cost and power, but we also get performance out of it, because it’s all so tightly coupled,” Thusoo said.

Arithmetic utilization for AI accelerator cores can be higher with sufficient memory bandwidth, but memory capacity can also play its part; in Tsavorite’s design, utilization actually improves with scale-out since resources can be shared between many chiplets when needed. Resources like memory appear unified to the software layer.

Chiplet designs

Tsavorite has developed two chiplets: OmniFlex and SkyFlex. OmniFlex has more computational power (more CPU and AI cores). On the other hand, SkyFlex has some acceleration, but it includes memory controllers that OmniFlex does not. A single SkyFlex is therefore the smallest possible configuration, and every combination requires at least one SkyFlex.

Tsavorite has developed four packages for different applications: T0, T1, T2 and T3. The T0 is the smallest, without any OmniFlex chiplets. The T1, with one OmniFlex chiplet, is designed to go up against Nvidia Thor in robotics applications. Both Tsavorite’s chiplets can handle SLAM acceleration, Thusoo said, and the T1 includes a functional safety island. Neither SLAM nor functional safety use much extra silicon area, he added, noting that including these features is more a question of the added complexity.

The T2 and T3 are designed for rack-scale deployments. Fifty racks of T2s have a footprint of about 12.5 MW, including 620 EFLOPS compute (FP4, AI cores only) and 9.6 PB DRAM with a total bandwidth of 31 PB/second. This is sufficient to train a 70B model on 15 trillion tokens in 27 hours, or inference the same model with throughput of around 360 million tokens per second. Pre-fill and decode stages can be separated either by virtually ringfencing resources within a package, or by changing the balance of OmniFlex versus SkyFlex chiplets in each package. The exact balance will depend on whether time-to-first token or number of users are most critical to the customer, Thusoo said.

The bigger T3 is designed to go up against Nvidia Rubin-generation rackscale hardware, competing on cost, power and throughput for inference, Thusoo said.  

The T0, T1 and T2 have LPDDR and the T3 uses HBM.

CPU plus AI

Within the OPU are in-house developed AI co-processor cores, plus Arm Neoverse CPU cores. The ratio of accelerator cores to CPUs is not 1:1, Thusoo said.

“[The Arm core] is not just managing [the AI core]—we think every general-purpose application will have an AI component to it going forward, so that’s why having the highest performance Arm core next to the highest performance AI core gives a lot of flexibility on the applications customers can run on our hardware,” he said.

The decision to go with Arm cores was based on software compatibility with Nvidia, and other data center software ecosystems.

“We looked at RISC-V, but it’s taken 20+ years to get server-class software going on Arm, so we wanted to take advantage of that,” Thusoo said.

The in-house AI accelerator is also designed to make the software transition from CUDA easier, which Thusoo said is partly at the expense of hardware complexity.

Tsavorite’s software stack, Taos, is designed to be CUDA-compatible. Taos enables reinforcement learning and fine-tuning as well as inference—an unusual choice given the added software complexity required and the size of the inference-only market.

“We feel there’s a lot of proprietary data in the world that people will not want to [put in the cloud], so we think fine-tuning will be critical,” Thusoo said. “For people to do fine-tuning on one piece of hardware, and then run inference somewhere else, that’s a barrier for customers. That’s what people think they’ll do, but in practice, they like to do it on the same hardware.”

The intention is not to support full-scale training, Thusoo said, but Taos today can handle reinforcement learning and fine-tuning, which the company believes are critical in the enterprise market.

Early customers have verified Tsavorite’s technology on its FPGA prototype. The company is working with OEM partners to build system-level products. Pre-production systems will be with early customers in mid-2026, with the first systems reaching general availability by the end of 2026.

data.title: Arm’s DreamBig Acquisition Reignites In-house Chip Prospects
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

DreamBig Semiconductor, featured in this year’s edition of EE Times’ “Silicon 100” list of startups to watch for their chiplet technology, has been acquired by Arm. The revelation came buried in Arm’s latest earnings report, in which the British semiconductor IP supplier agreed to acquire DreamBig for $265 million in October 2025.

“The core technology centers on the MARS chiplet platform, which includes the Chiplet Hub technology with high-bandwidth memory DRAM,” the Silicon 100 report noted in DreamBig’s profile. The Chiplet Hub technology integrates 3D HBM on top of a high-performance fabric base die, providing designers with centralized control of shared hardware resources across attached chiplets.

Silicon 100 also mentioned the company’s high-performance accelerator solutions for AI and data centers. DreamBig’s network accelerator chip incorporates a remote direct memory access (RDMA) engine to support 800 Gbps of bandwidth and 800 Mpps of throughput for intensive AI workloads.

The above product outline leads to two semiconductor design offerings. First, its chiplets IP, called Chiplet Hub, enables developers to create multi-die architectures with quad-sided UCIe die-to-die connectivity. Case in point: Athos Silicon has incorporated Chiplet Hub technology in its inaugural chip, code-named Polaris, to develop a chiplet-based platform for automotive, avionics, robotics and space applications.

Second, DreamBig’s monolithic chip, Mercury AI-SuperNIC, first unveiled in January 2025, allows AI chips like GPUs and TPUs to connect via a chiplet-compatible component, while providing up to 12.8 Tbps of bandwidth. It enables direct GPU-to-GPU interconnection by employing a remote DMA engine.

The Mercury chip features a hardware-accelerated RDMA engine that supports both RDMA over Converged Ethernet v2 and the new Ultra Ethernet Consortium standards. DreamBig claims that the Mercury chip redefines 800 Gbps discrete networking for GPUs and TPUs by combining performance, power efficiency and cost-effectiveness for next-generation AI platforms.

What Arm wants?

Arm, aiming to capitalize on surging AI and data center demand, has been seeking initiatives in areas like chiplets and networking. For instance, its Chiplet System Architecture provides a set of standards for system partitioning and chiplet connectivity to address fragmentation in this emerging semiconductor design realm.

DreamBig’s semiconductor offerings, built on open standards, encompass both physical silicon and IP licenses. That poses a fundamental question regarding this acquisition: will Arm merely license DreamBig’s design offerings to its customers likeBroadcom, Marvell and Nvidia?

Here, it is important to note that DreamBig’s AI networking chips overlap with the AI silicon offerings of Broadcom, Marvell and Nvidia. That raises the question: will Arm consider commercializing DreamBig’s designs as full chips, as well?

A recent Reuters story hinted at the possibility of Arm transforming from a sole designer of processor IPs to also selling its own chips with a focus on driving AI enablement in the data center. If that is the case, could DreamBig’s Mercury networking chip become a stepping stone in this push toward developing and selling Arm’s own chips?

While industry watchers are keenly weighing this possibility, Arm’s recent courtroom spat with Qualcomm further adds weight to this premise. According to industry reports, Arm is competing with Qualcomm for data center processors supply to Meta Platforms, the owner of Facebook.

A chiplets technology underdog

Sohail Syed, an engineering alumnus of NED University in Karachi, Pakistan, co-founded DreamBig Semiconductor in 2019. Syed, formerly a senior director of engineering at Marvell Semiconductor, had founded another startup, FIRQuest, in 2015, which was acquired by Corigine in 2019.

Steve Majors is another prominent figure in DreamBig’s executive team. Majors, who believes that the startup’s accelerator is setting the bar for the next-generation AI networking, is a former Intel engineering director and now serves as Senior VP of Engineering at DreamBig Semiconductor.

DreamBig, based in San Jose, Calif., has a talent pool of more than 200 design engineers in Karachi, Lahore and Islamabad. The acquisition by Arm is a boost for the IC design ecosystem in Pakistan in general and NED University’s Tech Park specificcally, where DreamBig has set up a major design engineering outpost.

As for Arm, it shows how the SoftBank-owned IP behemoth is navigating its next move in the AI-centric design era. More details are likely to emerge in the coming days, which will determine how Arm plans to position this acquisition in the current AI-fueled momentum.

See also:

Arm Aims to Democratize Chiplets

Athos Spins Out Of Mercedes With Chiplet Concept for AVs

Twists and Turns as Qualcomm Wins Arm Legal Case, Arm Shares Rise

data.title: India Needs Component Depth and Skills to Support Manufacturing Momentum
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

New Delhi, India — India’s electronics manufacturing growth has entered a steady phase, but industry leaders say the country must close gaps in components, design depth and skilled manpower to move toward its long-term targets.

At India Mobile Congress 2025 last month, industry and government leaders said that India has the market strength and policy support to expand, but the pace of progress over the next few years will shape how far the country can go.

Zetwerk Electronics president Josh Foulger said India has strong momentum across mobile, telecom and electronics production. He pointed out new hubs coming up in Chennai, Bengaluru, NCR, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh and Andhra Pradesh, and said these states have the scale to support long-term capacity. “The next five years are going to be very important,” Foulger said. “We just need to stay focused and execute.”

While the growth is visible on the ground, India is still working through gaps in the component supply chain. Much of the manufacturing continues to depend on imported kits. Analog Devices India managing director Vivek Tyagi said India has moved ahead in assembly and system-level production but needs to shift toward design-led work. He said that “design adds immense value” and the next stage of manufacturing should be built on design done in India for both domestic and global markets.

Ericsson India managing director Nitin Bansal added that the component gap is visible in telecom hardware. “The nanometer technology we need for telecom equipment is typically around four to five nanometers, whereas what is currently covered under domestic manufacturing is much higher, around twenty-four nanometers or more.” He opined that this gap would take time to close as suppliers develop the capability to meet global standards.

While India has the market and the opportunity there is a clear need to map what is produced locally and what is missing.

“We need to assess what is available in India right now, do a gap analysis to identify what more is needed and then look at where investments, upgrades or government interventions may be required,” Foulger said.

While products like antennas, enclosures and many mechanical parts have already been localized to a large extent, deeper electronic components will need more time and coordination across industry and government.

Sushil Pal, India’s Joint Secretary for the Ministry of Electronics and IT, said it is not realistic for Indian companies to invest in research and development for mature technologies that are already available globally. He agreed that companies need access to capital at lower cost, technology partnerships for mature processes and a workforce trained to work in industrial environments. “The government today is helping create business-to-business pathways with other countries,” he said.

The country, as of today, does not have a dedicated Bachelor of Technology program in electronics manufacturing. Students usually pick computer science or electronics and communication and then move into software or design roles.

Tyagi explained that a manufacturing-focused degree would create a steady base of engineers who can work directly on the shop floor and support the expansion of factories. “Just as demand created interest in the IT sector years ago, electronics manufacturing will become aspirational as companies scale and salaries rise,” he said.

Pal said the industry needs to take part in building local supply chains. He acknowledged that some suppliers may not meet every global benchmark early on or may be slightly more expensive, but even a small share of sourcing can help them scale. “Even allocating five to ten percent of sourcing to them can go a long way in developing the broader ecosystem,” he said.

He was clear to note that the government does not interfere in business decisions and is focused on creating policies and frameworks that let companies operate and grow.

The common view is that India has reached a point where industry, government and academia are moving in the same direction. While India has the market strength and the interest from global companies, the next phase will depend on skilled workers, available components and competitive production costs.

Experts have consistently opined that design-led manufacturing will define the next stage. However, the next few years will show how far India can go towards building a competitive manufacturing base.

data.title: SkyWater CEO Calls Company ‘Quantum Foundry’
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

SkyWater CEO Tom Sonderman told EE Times in an exclusive interview that his company is emerging as the “quantum foundry” just as TSMC has become the AI foundry for Nvidia and AMD.

SkyWater’s quantum-computing customers, which include D-Wave and PsiQuantum, are roughly doubling to seven this year, Sonderman told EE Times.

“We’re bringing what we call the quantum foundry—our technology-as-a-service model—to the market at a time when innovators are breaking through the quantum barrier to taking this out of the lab into a more high-scale environment,” Sonderman said. “We’re in a good spot with the capabilities we’ve built over several years with companies like PsiQuantum and D-Wave. They provide a great foundation as we bring more companies into our ecosystem.”

SkyWater deals with an array of quantum technologies. One of the company’s latest customers, SQC (Silicon Quantum Computing), is making spin-based qubits. D-Wave is doing quantum annealing. Another SkyWater partner announced this month, QuamCore, is making a quantum processor through large-scale integration, Sonderman said.

QuamCore, the pioneer of a superconducting quantum processor, has signed a multi-million-dollar contract with SkyWater focused on developing single flux quantum devices.

In March, PsiQuantum expanded its partnership with SkyWater to produce silicon photonic chips. The engagement supports PsiQuantum’s goal to deliver a commercially viable, error-corrected general-purpose quantum computer that scales beyond 1,000,000 qubits using silicon photonics.

Also in March, D-Wave and SkyWater claimed a historic milestone by demonstrating quantum supremacy in simulation with an article in Science that validated annealing quantum computing for real-world problems.

Quantum foundry

Sonderman says he has won quantum clients through SkyWater’s technology-as-a-service environment that is conducive to collaborative innovation.

“That results in custom technology development,” he said. “One thing about quantum right now is there’s a lot of different solutions that are attacking the quantum problem and having the flexibility to come into a foundry environment.”

Quantum companies like PsiQuantum have raised billions of dollars in recent months. Sonderman believes much of the money they spend will be to make the core capabilities that demonstrate quantum outcomes.

“All the modalities that companies are interested in pursuing, we can offer a solution to,” Sonderman added. “That’s what makes it unique. We have the flexibility that customers are looking for, but we’re also able to leverage a lot of our core capabilities to make these innovations happen at a faster pace.”

Entire architectures for quantum systems need to be created, noted SkyWater Senior VP Ross Miller.

“Each of them is different, whether it’s superconducting, or ion trapping, or spin-based, or others, but they all require microfabrication technology,” Miller said. “For some of them, it’s core processor units that we’re helping to fabricate. In others, it’s some of the peripheral microelectronics or superconducting architectures that surround some of their core processors.”

A lot of the quantum devices are done in a hybrid fashion.

“We would do a piece, maybe other people would do a piece,” Sonderman added. “But our virtual IDM approach is to not only do the front end, but we do the back end. By linking all those under a single supplier like SkyWater, the companies we work with, the design companies, it reduces not only their risk, but it allows them to solve problems quicker than when they’re dealing with multiple suppliers.”

Cryogenic chips

SkyWater makes microelectronics for the U.S. government that need cryogenic solutions, Sonderman said.

“We’re able to apply that to the quantum space as well,” he said. “Many applications operate at the millikelvin level.”

AI has soared because TSMC is the foundry for the top AI design companies, according to Sonderman.

“That’s essentially what we’re doing here with quantum in the U.S.,” he added. “We’re not only leveraging our relationship with the U.S. government—trusted manufacturing, compartmentalization of information, really protecting IP at its most stringent level—but also bringing a foundry capability to the quantum space so that these companies can leverage their investments in a way that maximizes the ROI.”

SkyWater is more than doubling its sales with the acquisition of a Texas fab from customer Infineon earlier this year. The older part of SkyWater that does not include the Infineon fab acquisition is likely to see revenues from its Advanced Compute business, which is primarily quantum, grow from about 10% in 2024 to about 15% in 2025, according to the company.

The new quantum business will grow by about 30% annually, leading SkyWater’s corporate growth rate, according to Sonderman.

Growth drivers

Aerospace and defense have been the mainstay of SkyWater’s business, but Sonderman expects quantum and advanced packaging to be new growth drivers.

The SkyWater CEO sees a chance to become an advanced-packaging leader.

“Advanced packaging is tied to a program we announced back in January of 2024 that involved standing up a fan-out wafer-packaging solution. For the first time, SkyWater is seeing significant contributions out of Florida with our advanced packaging,” Sonderman added. “Those solutions just don’t exist in the United States.”

For example, TSMC’s new fabs in Arizona have started making chips for customers like Nvidia, but the finished wafers still need to be shipped over the Pacific to Asia for packaging.

Advanced packaging will be a forward-looking strategy for a lot of the innovation in quantum, but also innovation in the U.S., Sonderman noted.

“Other than what Intel, TSMC and Samsung may offer at the extreme nodes, there’s really no one else offering advanced-packaging solutions for the broader community,” he added. “SkyWater firmly believes we can be the leader in advanced packaging as this decade unfolds. The U.S. should have a commitment to be the leader in advanced packaging as well because it’s really a chance now to reclaim the mantle of technology leadership but also because it’s going to be foundational to things like quantum.”

CHIPS Act

In hindsight, Sonderman said the U.S. CHIPS Act helped to build more than enough new chip fabs in the U.S. but failed to close a larger vulnerability in the domestic semiconductor supply chain: advanced packaging.

“There was very little talk about moving more advanced packaging to the U.S.,” he noted. “You don’t see a lot of OSATs other than Amkor talking about coming back to the U.S. That’s an area where SkyWater is going to lean into. We plan on being the leader in open-source advanced packaging.”

Top-two global chip packager Amkor told EE Times its first advanced-packaging facility in the U.S., located near TSMC Arizona, will start production in early 2028. That is after Apple, TSMC and Amkor announced a joint advanced packaging effort in December 2023. Most of the world’s chip-packaging capacity is in Asia.

In March this year, TSMC announced it would add $100 billion to its investment in the U.S., an expansion that would build three new chip fabs and two advanced-packaging facilities separate from Amkor at TSMC’s existing fab complex near Phoenix, Arizona.

America’s foundry

Sonderman said he is proving the mission that SkyWater has set out for, which is to be America’s foundry, and to secure America’s silicon foundation.

“We don’t really have a foundry industry in the U.S,” he said. “We have pockets of it, but other than SkyWater, the entities are either not pure play, like Intel, or they are foreign controlled, like GlobalFoundries, Tower and X-Fab.”

SkyWater can build a robust foundry capability in the U.S. without constructing new greenfield fabs, Sonderman said, specifically regarding the Infineon fab acquisition.

“The real investment in future greenfield should be around advanced packaging and specifically panel-related advanced packaging because that’s where the industry’s going. It’s how we can not only ensure that we don’t just make the chips here, but we package and actually produce them holistically within the U.S.”

E-beam front-runner

SkyWater sometimes makes audacious leaps. The company is the only one so far to buy an e-beam tool from Multibeam for direct writing chip designs on a silicon wafer without a mask.

“It’s a great collaboration,” Sonderman said. “The premise is very much aligned with our advanced-packaging strategy.”

The tool is also uniquely suited for quantum, he added.

“It’s early stage. First, you make the technology work, and then, you gotta make it productive. One of the unique things about our model is that we can be working with [Lam Research founder] David Lam and that group on a futuristic tool like the Multibeam platform while at the same time running volume for Infineon all in the same fab. We use a direct-write approach for our advanced-packaging solution, and having a faster platform to achieve that outcome, I think, is going to be critical.”

data.title: Tesla Considers Building ‘Tera Fab’ to Meet Future Chip Needs
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

An upbeat Elon Musk, with his newly approved trillion-dollar pay package, talked to a very select group of investors at the Tesla annual shareholders meeting yesterday in Austin, Texas, and told the audience he had chips on his brain and planned to build a “Tera fab” that could potentially produce a million wafer starts per month to meet chip demand for Tesla alone.

Musk said that all he could think about was chips at the moment. He also said that with his AI5 chip and the planned AI6 chip, he did not think existing suppliers (TSMC and Samsung) could meet demand. As a result, he would consider some collaboration with Intel and also building Tesla’s own “Tera fab” to meet demand for Tesla’s own products.

“The thing is that we actually have agreed to buy all the chips that are made from the fab. So, it’s basically a money printing machine for TSMC and Samsung. It’s like literally the faster you make the chips, the faster we send them money. But it’s still not going just fast enough,” Musk said during the shareholder meeting.

“So that’s why I think, as far as I can see, the only option is to go build some like very big chip fab. And then you go to solve memory and packaging too. But otherwise, you just tap out at whatever the chip production rate is. And so, I guess, Tera would be—you’d want to say it’s got to be at least 100,000 wafer starts per month size fab. And maybe that would be one of ten in a complex. So, it would ultimately be one million wafer starts per month,” he added.

To this, he got a giggle from the audience, to which he responded, “Yeah. Exactly. You can tell when it gets the giggle factor. That’s probably a good sign that we’re onto something special. But I wouldn’t be surprised if long term, it’s like a million wafers a month.”

Musk expects huge demand coming from the Tesla AI5 chip for the company’s AI software stack. Currently, this would be made in four places: TSMC Taiwan, Samsung Korea, TSMC Arizona and TSMC Texas.

Possible deal with Intel?

However, the challenge, he said, is how to make enough chips. “I have a lot of respect for the Tesla partners, TSMC and Samsung. Maybe we’ll do something with Intel. We haven’t signed any deal, but it’s probably worth having discussions with Intel. But even when we extrapolate the best-case scenario for chip production from our suppliers, it’s still not enough,” Musk said.

This is the reason he believes that Tesla may need to build a Tera fab. While he did not give a timeline, he alluded to plans for a fab in the future.

“I can’t see any other way to get to the volume of chips that we’re looking for. So, I think we’re probably going to have to build a gigantic chip fab. Got to be done.”

Optimus driving chip demand

The key driver for demand would be from areas like robotics.
