Early in the shareholder meeting, Musk danced along with two Optimus humanoid robots at the sides of the stage and enthused about the transformation they would make to humanity, in terms of personalized healthcare, tackling crime and generally growing the world economy by a factor of ten.

Elon Musk believes the opportunity from the company’s Optimus robots will be huge, and said it’s going to be the biggest product of all time by far, bigger than cell phones, “bigger than anything.” (Source: Tesla)

Talking about the opportunity from Optimus, he said, “I think it’s going to be the biggest product of all time by far. So, like, bigger than cell phones, bigger than anything. I guess the way to think about it is that every human on earth is going to want to have their own personal R2-D2 C-3PO. So, who wouldn’t?”

On their usefulness in healthcare, Musk said, “With humanoid robots, you can actually give everyone amazing medical care. In terms of Optimus, [it] will be more precise. Optimus will ultimately be better than the best human surgeon with a level of precision that is beyond human. So, I think that’s a pretty wild concept to say, okay, you…there’s always people always talked about eliminating poverty, but actually, Optimus will actually eliminate poverty. Optimus will actually give people incredible medical care.”

And how is it useful in crime? Well, Musk’s vision is that if somebody has committed a crime, a more humane form of containment of future crime would be to give the offender a free Optimus robot, which would be programmed to just follow you around everywhere and stop you from doing crime.

“It’s just going to stop you from committing crime. That’s really it. You don’t have to put people in like prisons and stuff, I think.”

Musk added that everyone would want an Optimus robot, and that there could be tens of billions of Optimus robots on earth. He said it would launch the fastest production ramp of any product ever, with a one-million-unit production line in Fremont and a 10-million-production line in Austin.

AI5 chip: a third of the power of Blackwell and 10% of the cost

With chips on his brain, Musk talked quite a bit about the Tesla chip design expertise and how the AI5 chip was specially designed for Tesla’s needs.  He said he would put a lot of time into the Tesla chip design in enabling a functional robot.

Tesla AI5 chip improvements over it’s AI4 chip. (Source: Tesla)

 “In order to have a functional robot, you have to have a great AI chip. And it needs to be an inexpensive chip, and it needs to be very power efficient,” he said. “So, we believe the AI5 chip will be probably about a third of the power of, say, something like a Nvidia Blackwell, which is a great chip, for roughly comparable performance and much less than ten percent of the cost. So, this is a chip that is very much optimized for the Tesla AI software stack. It’s not meant to be a general-purpose chip. It’s meant to be an amazing chip for the Tesla AI software.”

Musk added that he thought Tesla is unique in using an integer-based system.

“Integer operations are fundamentally more efficient than floating point operations. So, we can do floating point, but the vast majority of our inference is done in integer, which is, if you’re familiar with sort of logic gates, the simplicity of integer, integer is much more power efficient, much more silicon efficient,” he said at the shareholder meeting. “But you actually have to train for integer inference, which everyone else is training for floating point. That’s kind of a niche technical detail, but it’s actually very important.”

A convincing plan?

While some members of the audience may have giggled with a certain sense of disbelief on Musk’s plans to build a Tera fab or fabs with a capacity to do a million wafter starts per month, it is not beyond imagination to envision a scenario that AI demand is so huge and driven by applications like his robots that he will need more capacity than TSMC and Samsung can offer now. A collaboration with Intel on its latest process technology could be a solution, but if Musk really believes that there is going to be demand for billions of robots, one can see why he is putting it out there now that Tesla will need to build its own fabs.

We will be watching to see when there is a timeline put on this—there may already be one in Musk’s head, we will just have to wait to see when he decides the time is right to reveal more details of funding, locations and targets.

The full video recording of the Tesla 2025 shareholders meeting can be found here.

See also:

Samsung’s Multi-Billion Tesla Deal

Infineon OktoberTech 2025: Humanoid Robotics, Edge AI and Power

Tesla’s wireless-power “dream” gets closer to reality—maybe

Will Robots Use Reasoning At The Edge?

Inside Robotics Power: Safety, Batteries, and Market Growth

India’s Addverb Brings AI and Edge Computing to Warehouse Robotics

data.title: Eta Compute Rebrands as ModelCat, Unveils Agentic Model Builder
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

SANTA CLARA, Calif. – AI software startup Eta Compute has rebranded as ModelCat and unveiled a hardware-aware model building platform for edge devices that uses agentic AI. The company has also partnered with NXP to bring an NXP-specific version of the tool into NXP’s eIQ software ecosystem.

ModelCat’s tool uses an AI agent to orchestrate the model creation process, automating most of the work to produce a model tailored to the constraints of the selected edge hardware it will run on, Evan Petridis, CEO of ModelCat, told EE Times.

“The promise of AutoML was that it would generate a model for you, but it was really just a bunch of libraries,” Petridis said. “If you’re an expert, you could use them—it would get you further down the road, but [it] still needed people in the loop.”

ModelCat does use some of the same techniques as older AutoML techniques did, Peditris said.

“The key is it remains grounded,” he said. “Because [our agent] has access to the actual hardware, it can test models, it can hypothesize and come up with ideas about what would be a good architecture or whatever, but then instead of suggesting ideas, it can actually go and test those suggestions.”

ModelCat has various hardware from companies including NXP, STMicroelectronics, Silicon Labs, Qualcomm and others in its cloud so its agent can test on real hardware.

“Results are presented not as ‘I tried this and it didn’t work,’ but as, you gave it a problem, how well has it solved that problem?” Petridis said. “It gives you metrics about things like prediction accuracy, but also hardware-specific metrics like latency, variance in run time and energy consumption.”

ModelCat’s tool uses an AI agent to reduce a manual, iterative process to a single step, Petridis said, reducing what he called “nested loops” in the development cycle.

ModelCat’s demo showed its tool working with open-source or customer datasets. The designer specifies which hardware they are working with, then decides on other constraints like latency or energy requirements, then presses a button to start the process. The agent will consider similar datasets and models that worked well in those scenarios to make an initial choice of model architecture, then it tests its hypothesis by running inference on real hardware. It can conclude that the network size needs to be reduced (by pruning, which it can do itself) or that a different architecture would be better, in which case it starts the process again. Once it is satisfied, it trains the selected model architecture on the user’s data. Results, including latency and energy per inference for different models, are presented on a single graph. The user can then select one to download and use.

Today, ModelCat’s servers offer various edge hardware from companies including NXP, STMicro, Silicon Labs, Synaptics, Qualcomm and others. Some of these companies are ModelCat partners, but partnership is not necisarially required, Petridis said, since the company’s system discovers the limitations of new hardware types itself.

“The way the product is structured today is that you already know what hardware you want to use,” Petridis said, noting that it is nevertheless possible to begin the process again for a different chip and compare results manually.

The key criteria to hardware choice at the edge today is legacy software, Petridis said, as it binds users to a particular vendor for generation after generation of chips. Hardware suppliers see portability as the enemy today, he noted, adding that this was solved for NXP by making a separate version of the tool that only offers NXP hardware targets for the eIQ ecosystem.

data.title: Cost-Efficient AI at Scale is a Software Problem
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

SANTA CLARA, Calif. – Cost-efficient AI at scale is a software problem, given that all Nvidia competitors are lagging behind on software development. Not only that, but software will also become even more critical as heterogeneity in the data center becomes commonplace, Moreh CEO Gangwon Jo told EE Times in a recent interview.

South Korean startup Moreh was formed by a group of HPC experts in 2020 to build an AI software stack for AMD GPU clusters. AMD gaming GPUs were the cheapest way to build a supercomputer at the time, Jo said, but the AI software stack was missing. Moreh was founded to address this gap, with the overall aim of making AI training and inference at scale cost-effective by enabling alternatives to Nvidia hardware.

“Our first goal was to provide software equivalent to Nvidia’s CUDA for AMD GPUs,” Jo said. “We built the entire software stack by ourselves because AMD’s ROCm was very immature at that time. We needed to implement everything by ourselves, from libraries to the upper-level framework.”

Moreh installed a cluster of 2,000 AMD GPUs for Korea Telecom (KT) in 2021, which at the time was the biggest AMD GPU-based AI infrastructure in the world, according to Jo. This cluster was used to provide public cloud services for more than 100 Korean customers, and for KT’s internal AI model development. By 2023, Moreh’s stack was able to train a Korean LLM using KT’s data on their 1,000 AMD MI250 GPUs.

AMD noticed; the GPU giant invested in Moreh’s Series B in 2023. Today, Moreh is a software partner to AMD, helping support Asian customers. Moreh also resells AMD hardware.

In 2025, Moreh has become more focused on inference at scale, building an inference stack for AMD GPUs from the libraries up to cluster-level support. Moreh’s stack was migrated to run on top of ROCm in 2023, which allows the company to focus on cluster-level optimizations for MI300-generation hardware today.

“The ultimate goal is to make some cluster-level software that is independent of specific hardware,” Jo said. “Our ultimate vision is focused on heterogeneous clusters to deliver the best cost efficiency for different workloads and applications.”

Heterogeneous clusters will become more common in the age of inference, Jo said. For example, different hardware is sometimes used for transformer pre-fill and decode stages, or to provide different context lengths and model types like agents.

Moreh aims to automate some cluster-level tasks. For example, the company can present large clusters as a single large GPU to the user, who no longer needs to manually split models or jobs across the hardware. Moreh’s software automates parallelization and optimization for the target cluster. While this is already up and running for training tasks, the company is working on a distributed inference version of this technology targeted at second-tier data center operators in Asia and beyond.

Business model

Moreh is still exploring potential business models, Jo said. The company will license its software stack to interested companies, but Jo said this business model has proven challenging because, in his words, “customers usually do not want to pay only for software.” As an AMD reseller, Moreh prefers to sell hardware and software as a bundle.

“We are also doing end-to-end deployment, not only reselling, but also consulting and optimizing based on customers’ real-time scenarios,” Jo said.

In order to fully understand model evolution and changing applications and use that knowledge in its infrastructure products, Moreh has had an internal model development team over the last couple of years. It has now spun this team out as Motif. Motif released a 2.6B LLM in the summer; the team plans to release a 10B version this year, and a 100B version next year. Initial models are LLMs but they are also working on video generation models, Jo said.

Cost-efficient

Moreh also works with Tenstorrent hardware today, with the same aim of providing a cost-efficient alternative to Nvidia.

“Tenstorrent’s hardware philosophy is good and fits our software philosophy,” Jo said. “Their single chip is weak—single-chip performance is much worse than Nvidia GPUs—but their strength is their network connectivity. We can install more chips in a scalable manner to match the same level of performance as Nvidia, but with lower cost.”

This hardware philosophy means more software work is required, but Moreh can solve these software problems itself (in other words, it doesn’t have to wait for Tenstorrent to do so), in part enabled by Tenstorrent’s commitment to open sourcing its software stack. This enables third parties like Moreh to build out the upper layers, though Jo said Moreh also modifies lower-level software.

Moreh plans to release a combined solution (Tenstorrent’s Galaxy servers plus Moreh’s training and inference stacks) before the end of 2025.

As a small company, Moreh is not looking for more hardware to work with today; the vision is to provide good software solutions for AMD and Tenstorrent. “Perhaps chip vendors will want to collaborate with us to support their chips inside our software,” Jo said.

SGLang collaboration

Moreh is collaborating with SGLang, an open-source serving framework for LLM and VLM inference. The companies plan to work together on distributed inference for AMD GPUs.

Surprisingly, a combination of many different techniques is required to achieve 1% or even 0.5% performance improvement in the data center today, Jo said, making even incremental improvements tricky to achieve. For this reason, Moreh does not want to compete with open-source projects.

“We want to combine our efforts with SGLang’s community to make an ultimate solution,” Jo said. “Then we could, for example, attach our cluster-level solution with the existing SGLang, to allow our company to focus on its strengths.”

DeepSeek optimization

Since DeepSeek emerged, Moreh has been supporting customers in China, many of whom have been working hard to optimize DeepSeek’s performance on non-Nvidia hardware. DeepSeek open sourced parts of its library, which helped, though it is Nvidia-specific. Part of Moreh’s work has been to use the same ideas to optimize DeepSeek for AMD hardware.

“DeepSeek presented lots of new ideas for optimizing inference into the community,” Jo said. “DeepSeek is actually the motivation behind the recent trend in distributed inference.”

Following DeepSeek’s lead, Moreh is working on implementing individual techniques and optimizing individual models for specific hardware, but it also wants to add more value at the higher levels by providing a solution that uses heterogeneous hardware. The company recently built a proof-of-concept system with two types of AMD GPUs, which Jo said achieved 50% performance improvement by applying proper disaggregation (by splitting the prefill and decode stages effectively, prefill on MI300 and decode on MI308x).

This is just the beginning of heterogeneity; it will come to all workloads, Jo said. Today, a common scenario is different generations of Nvidia GPU, but Moreh wants to make heterogeneous clusters cost-effective for a wider variety of hardware vendors and types.

“This kind of problem is not heavily addressed by the industry yet, so that’s the main value proposition of our company,” Jo said. “This is just the starting point.”

data.title: Nvidia Unveils Blueprint for Urban AI at SCEWC
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Accelerated compute and Digital Twins lay the groundwork for the ‘AI Factory’

Barcelona—At the Smart City Expo World Congress (SCEWC), city leaders focused on the practical implementation of high-performance artificial intelligence (AI) to transform public services.

Amid the brisk technological discussions at the SCEWC, a fundamental shift in urban management is underway, driven by accelerated computing and AI. Nvidia, leveraging its position as the “creator of accelerated compute,” is architecting what it terms the “brain of the city”—a central intelligence system designed to move local governments from perpetually reactive management to proactive, data-driven governance.

In an interview with EETimes, Charbel Aoun, Nvidia’s smart spaces and local government director for EMEA, stated that the company’s goal extends beyond hardware sales. Nvidia aims to democratize AI, making it “accessible and affordable and efficient for as many” industries as possible, including smart spaces and local government. This approach helps city leaders address urgent challenges, including population growth, traffic congestion, climate resilience, and real-time safety.

Three pillars of urban intelligence

Nvidia’s strategy focuses on three interlinked technologies: computer vision, generative AI, and digital twins. Mr. Aoun emphasized that these technologies are “significantly more powerful when combined.”

Computer vision, which began with perception AI for tasks such as identifying cars, fires, or smoke, now integrates with generative AI to provide advanced capabilities. Aoun noted the development of video large language models (VLM), which enable city officials to “talk to the videos of the cities.” This convergence introduces Agentic AI, in which specialized agents manage specific domains such as airport safety or traffic management.

The third pillar, digital twins, uses platforms like the Nvidia Omniverse to create high-fidelity replicas of the physical world that follow the laws of physics. By streaming real-world data into these twins, city leaders can collaborate, simulate, and analyze outcomes, supporting operations across factories, stadiums, roads, and train stations.

AI Factory and sovereign control

Cities have traditionally addressed technology challenges with “pinpoint solutions,” leading to siloed data and reactive management. Nvidia recommends that municipalities establish an “AI factory” that processes raw data, such as camera feeds, within a sovereign environment. This ensures data remains under city control and complies with local regulations, including the EU framework.

This sovereign approach is crucial, allowing cities to localize and fine-tune global AI knowledge to address specific, persistent urban issues. Aoun provided striking real-world examples of how this intelligence system solves complex problems quickly and affordably.

He described a city mayor facing protests from animal activists over horse-drawn carts operating during peak heat hours. The mayor needed to enforce restrictions between 12 p.m. and 4 p.m. Instead of developing an expensive custom solution, the AI factory provided immediate functionality. With VLM and city intelligence, the city could instruct the system: “When you see horses between 12pm and 4pm, take a picture of the horse and the plate number and inform me.” This enabled officials to launch an educational campaign and later enforce penalties. The approach significantly reduces deployment time and cost, allowing cities to address issues “faster, cheaper, in a sovereign way under your control at fractional cost.”

Ecosystem as strategy

Nvidia maintains a unique business model in this space. They do not engage in direct commercial deals with cities or banks; instead, they act as “a facilitator and an AI advisor,” servicing customers exclusively through their massive partner ecosystem. This network encompasses roughly 1,000 startups focused on smart spaces and local government solutions.

This collaborative model also supports economic development and talent retention, key concerns for city leaders. Mayors often note that graduates leave for better opportunities. By establishing an AI factory, offering programs and training such as Nvidia’s Deep Learning Institute, and connecting with startup hubs, cities can “create the foundation of a very vibrant startup community,” Mr. Aoun explained.

Partner-built solutions leverage Nvidia’s foundational technology to create impact across multiple domains. For example, SNCF (the French rail) works with partners such as Akila 3D and Think Deep to integrate cameras and digital twins into train stations, resulting in energy savings and optimized passenger flow.

SNCF Gares&Connexions is working with Akila to optimize and future-proof its network of train stations and railways across France and Monaco—operating nearly 14,000 trains daily.

Nvidia’s success depends on market adoption. Aoun stated: “We help the demand side, which is the cities and the consumer. We understand their problem, and we go to the supply side between us and the ecosystem, and we find solutions.”

If cities succeed, they will adopt and scale the technology, purchasing the necessary compute and software from the open market. Ultimately, this interconnected technology stack is transforming cities into sophisticated platforms—AI factories—that convert large volumes of raw data into actionable intelligence for building inclusive and resilient communities.

data.title: Applied Brain Research Demos First Silicon For State-Space Models
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Neuromorphic chip startup Applied Brain Research (ABR) has received samples of its first commercial hardware: a state-space network accelerator. The company has A0 silicon up and running in their lab, with an automatic speech recognition application running in 35 mW with 120 ms latency and 10% word error rate. The design target for an optimized version of this application is 30 mW, ABR CEO Kevin Conley told EE Times.

“Our goal is to get full speech recognition running at 30 mW, and with that, you would be able to run for an entire work shift on a single 200 mAh battery,” Conely said.

Demo applications include a natural language interface for a vehicle climate control system, which runs ABR’s speech-to-text and natural language processing models concurrently on the same chip.

ABR’s secret sauce is hardware-algorithm codesign using state-space models, a type of neural network that uses many few parameters than transformers and other types of models. State space models were developed by Chris Eliasmith’s group at the University of Waterloo in 2018; Eliasmith co-founded ABR to commercialize them, and serves as ABR CTO today.

“The novel part of a state-space network is that we’re combining linear and non-linear processing layers, which are very good at compressing time representation,” Conley said.

Processing time-series data like audio with transformers is complex since the transformer needs to remember context and recompute it every time new data arrives. Instead, state-space networks compress time into a state vector (a compressed, higher dimensional representation of time), which enables more efficient forward prediction.

“In the tests that we’ve done, comparing against transformers, [state-space models] perform at the same level as the transformer with half the number of parameters,” Conley said.

A vector representation of time is particularly efficient because as context scales (as the amount of data increases), the compute required increases linearly. Transformers need to manipulate matrices, so their complexity grows quadratically.

“State-space networks are inherently real-time,” Conley said. “Because we’re keeping this compressed representation of time, we can always be updating it. We don’t have to wait for a bunch of history to happen and then compute over the whole thing to get an accurate readout, which is great if you’re doing real-time speech transcription where you want the transcription to start right when you start speaking.”

State-space chip

ABR’s chip is designed to accelerate state-space networks efficiently. It has a small CPU to manage dataflow through its accelerator, next to a DSP for data pre- and post-processing. The accelerator itself is a linear dataflow engine, with its hardware-specific compiler also reflecting significant development time. This compiler builds efficient sequences for dataflow processing. The stack can handle data curation, training and customization of ABR models, quantization to 8-bit or 4-bit, and profiling of algorithms on ABR hardware. Multiple models can run in parallel/cascading, and they can be state-space models, or not (another demo showed a state-space model used alongside a small CNN, which will run perfectly well with comparable performance to other types of hardware, Conley said).

Parameter count, limited by on-chip memory, is 10 million for INT8 (since the chip has 10 MB SRAM), or 20 million INT4 parameters. The company’s ASR model, for example, has 12 million parameters (mixed precision 8/4-bit), leaving plenty of room for other models to run concurrently.

For its first commercial product, ABR has implemented its dataflow core in 22-nm ULL process technology in a 4.2×4.2 mm package to suit applications like AR/VR glasses, but the architecture could be scaled up for future products, Conley said.

Commercial traction so far has come primarily from AR/VR applications where there is strong interest in voice technologies, Conley said.

“There’s a lot of resurgence around [voice] in this sector,” he said. “Robotics is the second hottest area where we’re seeing interest, for home robots and robot pets, but also for industrial robotics use cases which are very interesting.”

Industrial use cases could include voice control of devices. Conley’s example is a quality engineer using both hands to measure something, then recording the result via speaking it out loud into an edge device of some kind. Some interest is also coming from the automotive industry, he said, as well as from non-voice time-series data applications like biosensing.

“There’s a lot of really interesting things you can do once you expand your context window from a few seconds to a few hours,” Conley said. “There are a few interesting companies doing ground-breaking work on wearable products that are going to be able to help people detect situations where they need clinical intervention. We’re getting a lot of interest in our models for that, and our chips.”

ABR will license its state-space models to customers today to enable them to get up and running on conventional hardware, but the ABR chip will run them at “a hundred times less power,” even versus other edge chips, Conley said.

ABR’s chip is expected to reach full production in Q2 2026.

data.title: Brain-Controlled Interfaces Redefine Human–Machine Interaction
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

When two Indian engineers set out to build assistive robotic devices in 2019, they discovered that even the most advanced prosthetic arms were unintuitive to use. Their early experiments led them to use brain signals instead of muscle signals to control movement. That experiment set the genesis for Nexstem, a startup founded in 2020 by BITS (Birla Institute of Technology and Science) Pilani alumni Siddhant Dangi and Deepansh Goyal, who developed a modular brain-computer interface (BCI) platform that allows developers and companies to create brain-controlled applications and devices.

Brain signals, or EEG signals, are generated by electrical dipoles in the brain. Nexstem works entirely on non-invasive systems, observing these weak, noisy signals externally. Once captured, the team uses algorithms to identify patterns. Nexstem’s platform comprises a non-invasive headset and a software development kit (called Wisdom SDK), which allows developers to perform real-time EEG analysis and build no-code interfaces that map brain activity to control systems.

“For instance, thinking about moving left or right produces distinct patterns,” said Nexstem co-founder Deepaansh Goyal in an exclusive interview with EE Times. The company’s Motor Machine module predicts a user’s intent to perform motor actions, like moving forward or backward or pushing and pulling objects, which can control wheelchairs or gaming avatars, creating more immersive human–machine interaction.

“We are creating the link between the EEG signal received by our headsets and the software needed to process and map this data to a multitude of applications,” said Nexstem CEO Siddhant Dangi in a press statement.

Nexstem is in the process of applying for a license from the Central Drugs Standard Control Organization and plans to apply for U.S. FDA approval early next year. The startup is currently working with Indian government-backed projects and had represented India in a medtech conference in Japan earlier this year.

Goyal said that Nexstem provides an end-to-end solution, covering hardware, board design, programming and validation—all developed in-house. “Our USP is that anyone can build and deploy applications using Nexstem’s architecture. The signals we work with are about one to ten microvolts, while the noise is in millivolts. We built our own analogue systems to capture such small signals cost-effectively,” he said.

“Typically, building such a system would take a company two to three years and about 5–7 million dollars of R&D. With Nexstem, they can achieve the same in four to twenty-four weeks,” he added.

Developing a cost-effective, high-fidelity BCI requires multiple design iterations. “We use tools such as Siemens’ HyperLynx and Expedition for board design and rely on Analog Devices and Texas Instruments for components,” Goyal said.

After 10-12 prototypes, the team refined the architecture. The startup has built technological barriers to deter replication, including custom analogue and digital systems and complex FPGA-based integrations. One challenge, Goyal noted, was the limited availability of high-quality ADCs for biosignals. “Unlike smartphones, where modular components are standardized, the biosignal infrastructure market is highly fragmented. We’re building the entire ecosystem from the ground up.”

Design and manufacturing strategy

Nexstem’s headset records EEG signals using a 19-channel sensor array with two active reference electrodes (Common Mode Sense/Driven Right Leg) that provide broad brain coverage with low electrode–skin impedance and minimal noise interference. The headset also offers about four hours of battery life over Wi-Fi or Bluetooth connectivity, with memory foam cushioning for comfort.

The design combines rigid, flex and rigid–flex PCBs to maintain compactness. The company maintains an in-house prototyping setup and uses contract manufacturers for large-scale production, about 90% of which is based in India. However, Goyal pointed out that Indian PCB manufacturers still face limitations. “Manufacturing complex 20- or 24-layer boards is difficult here. Most still use mechanical drilling instead of laser tools, which limits precision compared to Taiwan, Japan or South Korea.”

To scale, Nexstem aims to integrate small-scale manufacturing in-house.

“High-quality manufacturers usually only work with large-volume orders, leaving a gap for mid-scale requirements,” Goyal said. “In-house manufacturing will help us protect our IP and maintain control over production.”

Business model and customer base

Nexstem’s main customers include researchers, research laboratories and institutions like NIMHANS, IISc Bengaluru and several IITs. The company also has MOUs with universities and ongoing discussions with hospitals for pilots. “Our customers are bold innovators—anyone who wants to integrate human body signals into their product,” Goyal said.

The SDK allows users to develop BCI applications using a drag-and-drop interface powered by GPU-based algorithms for rapid computation. The company follows an IP-licensing model.  Three models are available: a free version, a $15/month local version and a $100/month cloud plan that removes the need for separate computing infrastructure.

“Companies take an annual license, and there is a royalty component,” Goyal said. Depending on customer needs, they can license Nexstem’s IP, define their product specifications and build their own variation within two to six months.

“We are not an ODM or IDH. If you wanted to build your own phone, you wouldn’t make the CPU, you’d license IP from Qualcomm,” Goyal clarified. “Similarly, we license our IP so that customers can integrate our modules and develop their own products.”

Customers can acquire sensors, computing modules and connectivity options based on whether they process data on the cloud or at the edge. “Edge computing ensures better data privacy since the user’s data remains on the device,” he added.

Elaborating further on data privacy, Goyal emphasized transparency. “Biopotential signals without context are meaningless,” he explained. “Our algorithm pipeline is fully transparent. Users can see what we do with their data, how it is stored, and they have full control to delete it whenever they wish.”

So far, Nexstem has raised around $5.7 million, sufficient to advance its R&D and expand its 29-member cross-domain team. “For a long time, we were just seven or eight people, and since we handled prototyping ourselves, the process has been quick and cost-effective,” Goyal said.

The next milestone is miniaturizing the technology for wearables and eventually building proprietary silicon. “We aim to partner with semiconductor companies such as TSMC, Samsung, or Qualcomm,” Goyal noted. The startup is seeking a lead investor in the U.S. who understands deep-tech and consumer electronics.

In the near term, Nexstem’s focus is on fulfilling commitments to customers across defense, healthcare, wellness, sports, gaming and entertainment. “For instance, a gaming company can use our Instinct headset to track how users feel during gameplay,” Goyal said. “Based on the user’s emotional state, a VR game could change its storyline dynamically.”

Building India’s neurotech foundation

Despite its progress, Nexstem operates in an ecosystem still in its infancy. “To be honest, there is barely any neurotech ecosystem in India,” Goyal said. “We are trying to build one ourselves.”

Each month, the company hosts Neurotech Round Tables, connecting researchers and founders to explore applications and collaborations. “Globally, neurotechnology has mostly stayed within research labs and hospitals. It never reached commercial deployment because of cost and machine size. We’ve solved about 80% of those challenges,” he said.

Goyal noted that education and venture capital interest remain limited, but awareness is growing, helped by companies like Neuralink. “When we began, about a year before Neuralink, there were only 100 to 200 research papers available on neurotech. That number is growing now,” he said.

As Nexstem moves toward miniaturization and chip-level integration, Goyal shared the company’s ultimate ambition was to evolve from a hardware and data company into a full biosignal infrastructure provider, forming the foundational layer for brain-controlled systems across industries.

Indian Startup Targets EMI/EMC Bottlenecks With Design-Stage Simulation

How EdgeQ Is Building India’s First Unified 5G + AI SoC Building Edge AI Processors With DigAn Technology

data.title: Manufacturing Contraction Quickens in October
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Economic activity in the U.S. manufacturing sector contracted for the eighth consecutive month in October. This extended period of decline follows a brief two-month expansion which was preceded by 26 straight months of contraction. Despite the manufacturing sector’s contraction, the overall U.S. economy continued its expansion for the 66th consecutive month.

The latest report from the Institute for Supply Management (ISM) indicates that while several demand indicators showed improvement, they remained in contraction territory, reflecting continuing economic uncertainty.

Susan Spence, Chair of the ISM Manufacturing Business Survey Committee, reported that the Manufacturing PMI registered 48.7% in October. This figure represents a 0.4-percentage point decrease compared to the 49.1% recorded in September. A reading below 50% signifies contraction in the manufacturing sector.

Spence noted that “In October, U.S. manufacturing activity contracted at a faster rate, with contractions in production and inventories leading to the 0.4-percentage point decrease of the Manufacturing PMI”.

Key report’s highlights:

  • The Manufacturing PMI registered 48.7%, indicating contraction for the eighth consecutive month, and at a faster rate than September.
  • The New Orders Index remained in contraction for the second consecutive month, registering 49.4%.
  • The Production Index returned to contraction, registering 48.2%, a decrease of 2.8 percentage points from September’s figure.
  • The Employment Index remained in contraction for the ninth consecutive month, registering 46.0%.
  • The Supplier Deliveries Index indicated slower deliveries for the third consecutive month, registering 54.2%.
  • The Inventories Index contracted at a faster rate, registering 45.8%.
  • The Customers’ Inventories Index registered 43.9%, remaining firmly in ‘too low’ territory for the 13th straight month. This status is usually considered positive for future production.
  • The Prices Index remained in expansion (increasing) for the 13th straight month, registering 58%, though at a slower rate than September.
  • The Backlog of Orders Index registered 47.9%, indicating order backlogs contracted for the 37th consecutive month.
  • The New Export Orders Index contracted for the eighth consecutive month, registering 44.5%.
  • The Imports Index remained in contraction for the seventh consecutive month, registering 45.4%.

Demand, output, and employment still challenged

Demand indicators showed mixed signals but remained in contraction territory in October. The New Orders Index contracted for the second month, registering 49.4%. However, all four demand indicators (New Orders, New Export Orders, Backlog of Orders, and Customers’ Inventories indexes) did show improvement in October. The Backlog of Orders Index continued its prolonged contraction in October at 47.9%, marking the 37th consecutive month.

Regarding output, the Production Index contracted in October, registering 48.2%. Only one of the six largest manufacturing sectors—Transportation Equipment—reported increased production. Spence observed that “A chain reaction of one-month index improvements started with New Orders in August and flowed to Production in September. In October, it manifested in a 1.7-percentage point increase in the Backlog of Orders Index. These short gains have not appeared to translate into sustained growth for the sector, a reflection of continuing economic uncertainty.”

The Employment Index continued its contraction in October, registering 46%. This marked its ninth consecutive month of decline. Panelists continued to indicate that managing head count is the norm, as 67% of panelists reported this strategy. Spence noted that “For every comment on hiring, there were 3.4 on reducing head counts.” Companies are accelerating staff reductions due to uncertain near- to mid-term demand, with layoffs and not filling open positions remaining the primary head-count management strategies.

Trade friction continue to impact the supply chain

Inputs—defined as supplier deliveries, inventories, prices, and imports—were mixed in October. The Supplier Deliveries Index indicated slower delivery performance for the third consecutive month, registering 54.2%. The Inventories Index contracted at a faster rate, falling to 45.8%.

The Prices Index remained in expansion (indicating raw material prices increased) for the 13th straight month, registering 58%. Spence commented that “The Prices Index reading continues to be driven by increases in steel and aluminum prices that impact the entire value chain, as well as tariffs applied to many imported goods.”

Export and import orders experienced continued declines. The New Export Orders Index contracted for the eighth consecutive month, registering 44.5 percent. This contraction reflects “ongoing trade friction and resulting diminished demand, as evidenced by the 57% of panelists’ comments citing soft demand due to tariffs and uncertain U.S. economic policy,” according to Spence. The Imports Index also remained in contraction for the seventh month, registering 45.4 percent, which Spence stated indicates “ongoing lower levels of demand due to tariff pricing.”

Outlook remains uncertain

The report highlighted an operating environment heavily influenced by trade policy and geopolitical uncertainty. While four key demand indicators showed improvement, the underlying manufacturing activity contracted at a faster rate in October. Only two of the six largest manufacturing industries—Food, Beverage & Tobacco Products; and Transportation Equipment—reported growth in October.

Regarding the prolonged backlog contraction, Spence emphasized that “Ongoing contraction in the Backlog of Orders index means that trade issues and other geopolitical tensions are still at play. Significant improvement shouldn’t be expected until those issues begin to recede.”

The high percentage of the sector’s GDP in strong contraction (registering a composite PMI of 45% or lower) reached 41% in October, up 13% from September, signaling overall manufacturing weakness.

data.title: Kerala Positions Design and IP at Core of Chip Strategy
data.description:

Kochi, Kerala — While Gujarat boasts about OSATs and fabs, the Indian state of Kerala is charting a different path with its design-first semiconductor roadmap that focuses on chip design, testing and IP creation rather than competing to build multi-billion-dollar fabs.

Unveiling the state’s Vision 2031 framework at ReCode Kerala 2025, Chief Minister Pinarayi Vijayan said Kerala aims to create half a million jobs in the IT sector within the state itself, capture 10% of India’s IT market share and grow the sector’s economic output to ₹4.41 trillion (about $49.7 billion) by 2031.

“Kerala, which once set benchmarks in land reform and education, is now extending that vision to technology,” Vijayan said. The plan includes new initiatives, such as the Kerala Semiconductor Mission, the AI Mission and the Future Technology Mission, to drive innovation and employment. Seeram Sambasiva Rao, the special IT secretary for Kerala, emphasized the importance of emerging sectors, as well as the need for missions to fast track the developments.

Professor Alex James, director of the Indian Institute of Information Technology and Management-Kerala, as well as Dean at The Kerala University of Digital Sciences, Innovation and Technology (Digital University Kerala), said the state’s draft semiconductor policy would prioritize product development, ease of innovation and multidisciplinary collaboration. “Analog, RF and mixed-signal design and development align best with Kerala’s strengths and resource constraints,” James said.

Industry experts present at the event wholeheartedly expressed their support for the new policy framework, adding that the state’s strength lies in design rather than fabrication. “Chasing foundry capacity may not be the best approach,” said P.V.G. Menon, an industry expert and the former president of the India Electronics and Semiconductor Association.

A 28-nm fab, Menon noted, costs approximately $11 billion and consumes enormous resources. “Instead of spending billions on bricks and water and considering the sensitive ecology of the state, Kerala should rather invest in design, testing, and product certification facilities,” he added.

Menon urged the state to utilize its strong academic base to build design-centric clusters and to expand Maker Village 2.0, which will include new hardware incubation centers across the cities of Wayanad, Kannur, Palakkad, Alappuzha, Thrissur and Kottayam. “This is an era of competitive federalism,” he added. “Kerala already has the talent and policy depth. It just needs to tell its story better.”

Professor Rajesh Zele of IIT Bombay, who earlier led design teams at MaxLinear, called the current phase the “hockey-stick point” of India’s semiconductor journey, driven by the global chip shortage wake-up call. He acknowledged the long pauses in India’s semiconductor efforts but stressed on retaining the IP within the country as the way to go forward. “Nearly 20% of the world’s design engineers are based in India. We design 3,000 chips a year, but the ownership leaves India. The answer lies in building chips in India, for India,” he said.

That effort is already visible in public R&D. Libin T. T., a scientist at C-DAC Thiruvananthapuram, said the agency has developed a series of VEGA processors (32- and 64-bit chips on 180-nm and 28-nm nodes) and is now moving to 7 nm. “All our SoCs are designed in India; only fabrication happens abroad,” he said. Under MeitY’s Chips-to-Startup program, CDAC is currently engaged in 91 projects with 88 startups and over 2,000 institutions.

Kerala, he added, could play a larger role by aligning its semiconductor mission with C-DAC’s design ecosystem and offering local prototyping support. “The pieces are in place,” he said. “What’s needed is coordination between academia, startups and government.”

The design-led vision is not just confined to the public sector but the private sector, as well. Rijin John, CEO of Silizium Circuits, showcased a communication chip “designed, architected and laid out entirely in Kerala.” He said the project demonstrates that fabless product design can thrive locally if startups, research institutions and government programs work in tandem. “The ecosystem exists. The challenge is to use it effectively,” John said.

While digital design dominates the conversation, Kerala could carve an early advantage in analog, power and MEMS devices, according to Yogan Senthilkumar, CTO of iVP Semiconductors. “MEMS fabrication is far less capital-intensive than CMOS,” Senthilkumar noted. “Automotive-grade mixed-signal and sensor chips are realistic targets that can bring investors here.”

He also pointed to gaps in India’s supply chain—probe-card and test-socket manufacturing, for instance—that could become opportunities for smaller players in the state’s industrial clusters.

Talent, however, remains the critical enabler. Sankalp Singh, University program manager at Synopsys, said access to EDA tools has expanded rapidly through government schemes, with Kerala ranking second nationwide in institutional participation. “EDA isn’t just software. It’s the backbone that lets startups build chips faster and cheaper,” Singh said.

Singh was part of the consulting team that developed the new All India Council for Technical Education VLSI curriculum, which introduces seven semiconductor verticals across disciplines. “We need every lab producing 200 engineers, not just one,” he said. “Semiconductors now span silicon, systems and software. Whatever your background, there is a role for you.”

Kerala’s approach indicates a broader shift in India’s semiconductor ambitions, switching from chasing fabrication megaprojects to building IP and design capability. With its strong academic ecosystem, affordable talent and expanding startup network, the state aims to anchor the “brain” end of the chip value chain.

As Menon summed it up, “Kerala doesn’t need to build the next Taiwan. It needs to build the next great idea that powers it.”
